source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Alfalfa — emissions_per_kg: LCA literature places irrigated US alfalfa at 0.2–0.4 kg CO2e/kg. P&N 2018 meta-analysis of 38,700 farms covers all major animal feed crops; alfalfa's low-emission profile is driven by nitrogen-fixation (reducing synthetic N fertilizer demand), perennial root system (low tillage), and high yield diluting per-kg fixed costs. Value of 0.4 kg CO2e/kg represents the upper bound for commercially irrigated alfalfa (irrigation energy is the dominant emission source in water-intensive production regions such as California's Central Valley).

source.title: FAO — Alfalfa Crop Information
source.note: Alfalfa — emissions_per_kg: FAO crop information confirms alfalfa's low GHG footprint relative to other forage crops due to biological nitrogen fixation (BNF) reducing synthetic N2O emissions and perennial growth habit reducing tillage-related CO2. Consistent with LCA studies reporting 0.2–0.35 kg CO2e/kg for rain-fed or minimally irrigated systems; 0.35 used as a conservative midpoint for globally mixed production.

Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Grass hay — emissions_per_kg: LCA literature places perennial grass hay at 0.15–0.35 kg CO2e/kg. P&N 2018 covers all major livestock feed crops; grass hay's low emission profile reflects its perennial growth (low annual tillage), minimal nitrogen fertilizer need relative to annual crops, and moderate yield. Emissions are dominated by fertilizer N2O (for improved pastures) and machinery fuel. Value of 0.3 kg CO2e/kg is consistent with LCA estimates for mixed US grass hay production.

source.title: FAO GLEAM — Global Livestock Environmental Assessment Model
source.note: Grass hay — emissions_per_kg: FAO GLEAM v3.0 provides feed-crop GHG intensities as part of livestock supply chain LCAs. Grass hay and pasture forages are consistently among the lowest-emission feed inputs due to perennial root systems, low synthetic fertilizer inputs, and high carbon sequestration in permanent grassland soils. GLEAM feed-crop estimates for perennial grasses range 0.15–0.3 kg CO2e/kg; 0.25 represents the global production-weighted average for improved permanent grassland hay.

FAO (2014) — Greenhouse Gas Emissions from the Fisheries and Aquaculture Sectors
source.note: Fishmeal — nutrition: Feed-grade fishmeal (low-temperature/steam-dried) proximate composition per gram: protein 0.60–0.70, fat 0.08–0.12, ash 0.15–0.20, moisture ~0.10. Human nutritional database values are not applicable (feed ingredient); macros derived from standard fishmeal product specifications reported in the FAO aquaculture sector documentation.

source.title: FAO (2014) — Greenhouse Gas Emissions from the Fisheries and Aquaculture Sectors
source.note: Fishmeal — yield_fraction: Set to 1.0 because fishmeal is used as-is in feed formulations with no further edible-fraction processing step; the entire product is the functional feed ingredient.

source.title: FAO (2014) — Greenhouse Gas Emissions from the Fisheries and Aquaculture Sectors
source.note: Fishmeal — emissions_per_kg: FAO fleet-level emission intensities for small pelagic capture fisheries (anchovy, sardine, herring) average 0.9–1.2 kg CO2e/kg landed fish for purse-seine operations. At a FIFO conversion of ~4.5 kg wet fish per kg fishmeal, vessel fuel emissions alone reach 4.0–5.4 kg CO2e/kg fishmeal. Adding fishmeal plant drying and processing energy (~0.5–1.0 kg CO2e/kg) gives a total range of 5–7 kg CO2e/kg. Central estimate 6.0 kg CO2e/kg used as a production-weighted midpoint across major fishmeal-producing nations (Peru, Chile, Denmark, Norway).

source.title: Pelletier & Tyedmers (2007) — Feeding Farmed Salmon: Is Organic Better? (Aquaculture)
source.note: Fishmeal — emissions_per_kg: Pelletier & Tyedmers (2007) LCA of Norwegian Atlantic salmon feeds reports cradle-to-feed-mill emissions for conventional fishmeal at 5.0–8.0 kg CO2e/kg, central estimate ~6.5 kg CO2e/kg. Variability driven by source fishery fuel efficiency, drying technology (steam vs. direct-fire), and geographic origin. Consistent with the FAO fleet-level estimate (source 74) of 6.0 kg CO2e/kg.

source.title: FAO (2014) — Greenhouse Gas Emissions from the Fisheries and Aquaculture Sectors
source.note: Fish oil — nutrition: Feed-grade fish oil is 100% lipid with no protein, fiber, or carbohydrate. Caloric density ~9.0 kcal/g (lipid energy standard). Saturated fat fraction approximately 27% of total fat for pelagic fish oil blends (anchovy/sardine/herring); EPA+DHA make up 25–35% of total fatty acids. Human nutritional database values are not applicable (feed ingredient); macros derived from standard fish oil product specifications in FAO aquaculture sector documentation.

source.title: FAO (2014) — Greenhouse Gas Emissions from the Fisheries and Aquaculture Sectors
source.note: Fish oil — yield_fraction: Set to 1.0 because fish oil is used as-is in feed formulations; the entire product is the functional feed ingredient.

source.title: FAO (2014) — Greenhouse Gas Emissions from the Fisheries and Aquaculture Sectors
source.note: Fish oil — emissions_per_kg: Fish oil is a co-product of fishmeal production at a yield ratio of roughly 1 kg oil per 5 kg fishmeal (by weight). Under economic allocation (oil valued at 2–3× fishmeal per kg due to omega-3 EFA content), fish oil receives approximately 35–55% of total upstream capture and processing emissions. Applying this allocation to the 5–7 kg CO2e/kg fishmeal footprint and scaling to oil yield gives ~2.5–4.0 kg CO2e/kg oil. Central estimate 3.5 kg CO2e/kg used, consistent with published aquaculture feed LCA studies using economic co-product allocation.

source.title: Pelletier & Tyedmers (2007) — Feeding Farmed Salmon: Is Organic Better? (Aquaculture)
source.note: Fish oil — emissions_per_kg: Pelletier & Tyedmers (2007) report fish oil at 3.0–5.0 kg CO2e/kg under economic allocation (central estimate ~4.0 kg CO2e/kg) from the same cradle-to-feed-mill boundary used for fishmeal. Economic allocation is applied because fish oil commands a significant price premium over fishmeal per kg, driven by its omega-3 EFA (EPA/DHA) content which is the primary limiting nutrient in high-performance salmonid feeds. Corroborates the 3.5 kg CO2e/kg central estimate from source 74.
